bytes.topic: REST — resource naming, HTTP verbs, status codes, idempotency
bytes.title: OpenAPI — Designing REST Resources, Verbs, and Status Codes That Actually Mean Something
bytes.body:
OpenAPI's `paths` object is where REST intent becomes contract. Resource naming should reflect nouns, never verbs — `/users/{userId}/orders` not `/getOrdersForUser`. Each path operation maps to a single HTTP verb, and OpenAPI enforces this by letting you define `get`, `post`, `put`, `patch`, and `delete` as sibling keys under a path item.

Idempotency matters for tooling: OpenAPI marks `get`, `put`, and `delete` as idempotent by convention, which retry-aware HTTP clients respect. Document this explicitly by setting `x-idempotent: true` on `post` endpoints that support idempotency keys (e.g. Stripe-style payment creation).

Status codes in OpenAPI's `responses` map are not suggestions — code generators and mock servers use them to produce typed response models. Distinguish `200 OK` (resource returned) from `201 Created` (with a `Location` header pointing to the new resource) and `204 No Content` (successful delete). Avoid catch-all `200` responses; each code should reference a distinct `$ref` schema so clients get accurate types per outcome.
bytes.code_snippet:
paths:
  /users/{userId}/orders:
    get:
      operationId: listUserOrders
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Paginated orders
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/OrderPage'
    post:
      operationId: createOrder
      x-idempotent: true
      parameters:
        - in: header
          name: Idempotency-Key
          schema:
            type: string
          required: true
      responses:
        '201':
          description: Order created
          headers:
            Location:
              schema:
                type: string
        '409':
          description: Duplicate idempotency key
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/Error'

bytes.topic: GraphQL — schema design, resolvers, N+1 with DataLoader, subscriptions
bytes.title: OpenAPI to GraphQL — Schema Design, Resolvers, N+1, and DataLoader Explained
bytes.body: Coming from OpenAPI, the mental shift to GraphQL starts at the schema. Where OpenAPI defines discrete paths with request/response shapes, GraphQL has a single typed schema as the contract — `type Query`, `type Mutation`, `type Subscription`. Each field maps to a resolver function, not a controller action. The N+1 problem hits hard when resolvers are naïve: fetching a list of `Post` objects then firing a separate DB query per `author` field — 1 list query + N author queries. DataLoader solves this by batching and caching: it collects all author IDs requested within a single event-loop tick, then fires one batched query. Unlike OpenAPI's polling or SSE patterns, GraphQL subscriptions use WebSocket transport via `graphql-ws`, with `subscribe` resolvers yielding async iterables. Schema-first design (SDL) is the OpenAPI equivalent of writing your spec first — define types, then wire resolvers. Use `graphql-tag` for query parsing and `@defer`/`@stream` directives for incremental delivery, which have no direct OpenAPI analogue.

bytes.topic: OpenAPI — spec-first vs code-first, generating clients, documentation
bytes.title: OpenAPI — Spec-First vs Code-First, Client Generation, and Docs That Actually Work
bytes.body:
Spec-first means writing your `openapi.yaml` before any implementation. Your API contract is the source of truth — teams can generate server stubs, client SDKs, and mock servers before a single endpoint exists. Code-first generates the spec from annotations or reflection at runtime, which keeps the spec in sync automatically but couples your contract to implementation details.

For client generation, `openapi-generator` and `@hey-api/openapi-ts` consume your spec and emit typed SDKs. With `openapi-ts`, you get a fully typed `client.ts` with request/response types derived directly from your `components/schemas`. This eliminates hand-written API wrappers entirely.

For docs, Scalar and Swagger UI both consume the spec via a single URL — but Scalar renders `$ref` chains, `oneOf`/`anyOf`, and examples far more cleanly. Pair with `redocly lint` in CI to enforce naming conventions (`camelCase` operationIds, required `summary` fields) before the spec ever reaches consumers.

Spec-first wins when multiple teams consume the API. Code-first wins for solo projects where the spec is a byproduct, not a contract.

bytes.topic: tRPC — end-to-end type safety, procedure types, React Query integration
bytes.title: OpenAPI vs tRPC — Skip Schema Gen, Get End-to-End Type Safety
bytes.body:
tRPC eliminates the contract-generation step that OpenAPI workflows depend on. Instead of defining a spec, running `openapi-typescript` or `openapi-generator`, and consuming generated types, tRPC shares TypeScript types directly from router to client — zero codegen, zero drift.

Procedure types map cleanly to REST semantics: `query` for reads, `mutation` for writes, and `subscription` for WebSocket streams. Each procedure is validated with Zod at runtime, giving you the same schema-enforcement guarantees as an OpenAPI request validator middleware — but colocated with your handler logic, not in a separate spec file.

React Query integration is first-class. `trpc.user.getById.useQuery()` returns the same `{ data, isLoading, error }` shape as vanilla React Query, but input and output types are inferred from your server-side Zod schema — no `components/schemas` wrangling, no stale generated files to commit.

The tradeoff is real: tRPC only works in TypeScript-only stacks. If you need a public API consumed by third-party or non-TS clients, OpenAPI remains the correct choice. For internal full-stack TypeScript monorepos, tRPC eliminates an entire class of type-drift bugs that openapi-typescript codegen introduces between spec updates.

bytes.topic: API Versioning — URL vs header versioning, backward compatibility strategy
bytes.title: OpenAPI — URL vs Header Versioning and How to Document Both
bytes.body:
OpenAPI 3.x has no native versioning construct — versioning is an API design choice you encode into your spec. URL versioning (`/v1/users`, `/v2/users`) is the most tooling-friendly approach: each version gets its own `servers` entry or a separate spec file. Header versioning (`Api-Version: 2024-01-01`) requires documenting a custom request header as a `parameter` at the path or global level with `in: header`.

For backward compatibility, use `deprecated: true` on individual operations or schemas rather than yanking them. Pair this with a `x-sunset` extension (Zalando convention) to signal removal dates. When evolving schemas, prefer additive changes — new optional properties don't break existing clients validated against the old spec. Use `allOf` to extend base schemas rather than mutating them.

For multi-version specs, `$ref` shared components from a common `components.yaml` and overlay version-specific deltas. The OpenAPI Overlay spec (v1.0 draft) formalizes this pattern — define a base spec and apply targeted `update` or `remove` actions per version without duplicating the entire document.
bytes.code_snippet:
# openapi.yaml — header versioning + deprecation pattern
paths:
  /users:
    get:
      parameters:
        - name: Api-Version
          in: header
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
            enum: ["2023-01-01", "2024-06-01"]
      responses:
        "200":
          description: OK

  /v1/users:
    get:
      deprecated: true
      x-sunset: "2025-12-31"
      summary: "[Deprecated] Use header versioning instead"
      responses:
        "200":
          description: OK

bytes.topic: gRPC — protobuf schemas, streaming types, when to use over REST
bytes.title: OpenAPI vs gRPC — When to Swap Your REST Spec for Protobuf
bytes.body:
OpenAPI contracts describe REST endpoints — paths, verbs, request/response shapes using JSON Schema. Protobuf `.proto` files serve the same contract role for gRPC, but enforce a binary wire format and auto-generate type-safe clients across languages. Where OpenAPI uses `$ref` to compose schemas, protobuf uses strongly typed field definitions with explicit field numbers that guarantee backward-compatible evolution without versioning your paths.

gRPC exposes four RPC patterns that OpenAPI's request/response model can't natively express: unary (identical to a REST call), server streaming (one request → N responses; ideal for live feeds or log tailing), client streaming (N requests → one response; chunked uploads), and bidirectional streaming (N:N; chat, collaborative editors).

Reach for gRPC instead of your OpenAPI-backed service when: binary serialization latency matters at scale, you need streaming without bolting on WebSockets, or internal polyglot microservices need generated clients instead of hand-rolled HTTP logic.

OpenAPI stays the right tool for public-facing APIs — browsers and third-party consumers expect REST/JSON. Use gRPC at the service-to-service boundary where you own both ends.

bytes.topic: Authentication — OAuth2, JWT in headers, API keys, mTLS
bytes.title: OpenAPI Security Schemes — Defining OAuth2, JWT, API Keys, and mTLS in Your Spec
bytes.body: OpenAPI 3.x centralizes auth definitions under `components/securitySchemes`, keeping your security contracts machine-readable and tooling-friendly. For JWT in headers, use `type: http` with `scheme: bearer` and `bearerFormat: JWT` — this signals to generators (Swagger UI, Speakeasy) to inject `Authorization: Bearer <token>`. API keys use `type: apiKey` with `in: header|query|cookie` and a `name` field matching the exact header or param. OAuth2 schemes declare the full flow: `authorizationUrl`, `tokenUrl`, and fine-grained `scopes` per flow (e.g., `clientCredentials`, `authorizationCode`). Apply them globally or per-operation via the `security` array, using an empty `security: []` on an operation to explicitly mark it public — don't rely on omission. For mTLS, OpenAPI 3.1 added `type: mutualTLS`, which signals certificate-based auth without encoding cert details in the spec itself. Combining schemes in a single `security` entry means AND logic; multiple entries mean OR. Scope enforcement and actual validation remain in your gateway or middleware — the spec is the contract, not the enforcer.
bytes.code_snippet:
components:
  securitySchemes:
    BearerAuth:
      type: http
      scheme: bearer
      bearerFormat: JWT
    ApiKeyAuth:
      type: apiKey
      in: header
      name: X-API-Key
    OAuth2CC:
      type: oauth2
      flows:
        clientCredentials:
          tokenUrl: https://auth.example.com/token
          scopes:
            read:bytes: Read byte content
            write:bytes: Publish bytes
    MtlsAuth:
      type: mutualTLS

security:
  - BearerAuth: []

paths:
  /bytes:
    post:
      security:
        - OAuth2CC: [write:bytes]
        - ApiKeyAuth: []
  /public/feed:
    get:
      security: []

bytes.topic: Pagination — cursor vs offset, total counts, infinite scroll patterns
bytes.title: OpenAPI Pagination — Modeling Cursor vs Offset and Infinite Scroll Contracts
bytes.body: OpenAPI doesn't enforce pagination, but your schema contract dictates how clients consume it. For offset-based pagination, expose `limit` and `offset` as query parameters with explicit `minimum`/`maximum` constraints, and include a `total` field in the response envelope — but recognize that `total` is expensive on large datasets and often stale by the time the client reads it. Cursor-based pagination is superior for infinite scroll: use an opaque `cursor` string (base64-encoded timestamp + ID composite), return `next_cursor` and `has_more` in your envelope, and never expose the cursor's internal structure in your schema description. Mark `total` as `nullable: true` and document in the field's `description` that it's omitted for cursor-based responses. Use `oneOf` on the response schema to formally differentiate the two pagination modes if your API supports both. For infinite scroll clients, the contract that matters is `has_more: boolean` — make it required, not optional. Document `deprecated: true` on `total` when migrating from offset to cursor. Link your `Link` header RFC 5988 behavior via `externalDocs`.
bytes.code_snippet:
components:
  schemas:
    CursorPage:
      type: object
      required: [data, has_more]
      properties:
        data:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: '#/components/schemas/Item'
        next_cursor:
          type: string
          nullable: true
          description: Opaque cursor; pass as `cursor` on next request.
        has_more:
          type: boolean
        total:
          type: integer
          nullable: true
          deprecated: true
          description: Omitted for cursor-paginated responses.

bytes.topic: Rate Limiting — per endpoint, per user, sliding window vs token bucket
bytes.title: OpenAPI Rate Limiting — Documenting Per-Endpoint, Per-User Policies with Sliding Window vs Token Bucket
bytes.body:
OpenAPI doesn't enforce rate limits, but it's the contract layer where you document them precisely. Start by defining reusable response headers in `components/headers`: `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset` (epoch seconds for sliding window) or `X-RateLimit-Reset-After` (seconds until refill for token bucket). Attach these to every `200` and `429` response object on rate-limited paths.

For per-endpoint policies, use `x-ratelimit` vendor extensions directly on the `PathItem` or `Operation` object — most API gateways (Kong, AWS API Gateway, Azure APIM) consume these to generate enforcement rules. For per-user scoping, pair `x-ratelimit-scope: user` with a `securityScheme` reference so the gateway keys the counter to the authenticated identity from the JWT `sub` claim.

Sliding window semantics map to `X-RateLimit-Reset` (absolute timestamp), while token bucket maps to `Retry-After` (relative delay). Document both `429` headers explicitly — clients need to distinguish them to implement correct backoff. Never omit the `Retry-After` header from your `429` response schema; it's part of RFC 6585 and most SDKs auto-handle it.
bytes.code_snippet:
components:
  headers:
    X-RateLimit-Limit:
      schema: { type: integer }
    X-RateLimit-Remaining:
      schema: { type: integer }
    Retry-After:
      schema: { type: integer }

paths:
  /v1/completions:
    x-ratelimit:
      scope: user
      strategy: token-bucket
      requests: 60
      window: 60s
    post:
      responses:
        '200':
          headers:
            X-RateLimit-Limit:
              $ref: '#/components/headers/X-RateLimit-Limit'
            X-RateLimit-Remaining:
              $ref: '#/components/headers/X-RateLimit-Remaining'
        '429':
          headers:
            Retry-After:
              $ref: '#/components/headers/Retry-After'
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/RateLimitError'

bytes.topic: Filtering & Sorting — query param conventions, GraphQL args, protobuf fields
bytes.title: OpenAPI — Designing Filtering & Sorting Query Parameters the Right Way
bytes.body: OpenAPI 3.x lets you formally describe filtering and sorting conventions in your `paths` using `in: query` parameters, giving clients machine-readable contracts for what's filterable and how. Define each filter as its own named parameter (`status`, `created_after`, `tag`) rather than overloading a generic `filter` string — this enables validation, documentation generation, and SDK auto-completion. For sorting, the de facto convention is `sort=field:asc` or `sort=-field` (minus-prefix for descending), and you can enforce it via a `pattern` constraint in your schema. Combine this with `$ref` components so filter schemas are reusable across endpoints. For range filters, use paired params (`price_min` / `price_max`) and mark them as non-required with `nullable: false`. Always document defaults explicitly using `default:` in the parameter schema so generated clients don't silently omit them. Swagger UI and Redoc will render each param as a distinct input, making the API self-documenting without extra effort.
bytes.code_snippet:
parameters:
  - name: status
    in: query
    schema:
      type: string
      enum: [active, archived, draft]
  - name: created_after
    in: query
    schema:
      type: string
      format: date-time
  - name: sort
    in: query
    schema:
      type: string
      pattern: '^-?(created_at|name|score)$'
      default: '-created_at'
  - name: limit
    in: query
    schema:
      type: integer
      minimum: 1
      maximum: 100
      default: 20

bytes.topic: WebSockets — connection lifecycle, heartbeat, reconnection, rooms
bytes.title: OpenAPI WebSockets — Lifecycle, Heartbeat, and Rooms Alongside Your REST Spec
bytes.body:
OpenAPI 3.x models HTTP semantics only — WebSocket connections sit outside the spec entirely. The upgrade handshake (`HTTP 101 Switching Protocols`) is the only touchpoint OpenAPI can describe. Annotate your upgrade path in your OpenAPI YAML with `x-websocket: true` and `x-channels` to enumerate room patterns (`/ws/rooms/{roomId}`), making the contract discoverable in Swagger UI even though it can't execute WebSocket calls.

At runtime, three patterns matter: heartbeat, reconnection, and rooms. Use `ws.ping()` every 30s and terminate on missed pong — the `ws` library fires `pong` events automatically. For reconnection, exponential backoff with jitter (`delay = base * 2^attempt + rand(0, 1000)ms`) prevents thundering herd after a server restart. Rooms map cleanly to dynamic channels: store a `Map<roomId, Set<WebSocket>>` server-side and broadcast with a simple loop.

In OpenAPI 3.1, document the `101` response on your `/ws` path with `x-websocket-channels` listing all room patterns. Pair this with an AsyncAPI 3.0 spec for full event schema coverage — OpenAPI owns the upgrade contract, AsyncAPI owns the message payloads.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — consistent error format across protocols, problem+json
bytes.title: OpenAPI + Problem+JSON — Defining Consistent Error Schemas Across Every Endpoint
bytes.body:
RFC 9457 (Problem Details) gives your API a machine-readable error contract. In OpenAPI 3.1, define a reusable `ProblemDetails` schema in `components/schemas` with the canonical fields: `type` (URI), `title`, `status`, `detail`, and `instance`. Reference it from every `4xx`/`5xx` response using `$ref: '#/components/schemas/ProblemDetails'` and set `content` to `application/problem+json` — not `application/json`. This forces generated clients to deserialize errors correctly and signals to consumers that all errors share one shape.

Extend for domain errors by composing `allOf`: your `ValidationProblem` adds an `errors` map on top of the base schema. Document this in `components/responses` as a named response (`BadRequest`, `NotFound`) and reuse those refs across operations — a single source of truth. When you regenerate SDKs with tools like `openapi-generator` or `kiota`, all error types map to typed exceptions automatically, eliminating ad-hoc string parsing of error payloads across REST, webhooks, and server-sent event error frames.
bytes.code_snippet:
components:
  schemas:
    ProblemDetails:
      type: object
      properties:
        type:     { type: string, format: uri }
        title:    { type: string }
        status:   { type: integer }
        detail:   { type: string }
        instance: { type: string, format: uri }
    ValidationProblem:
      allOf:
        - $ref: '#/components/schemas/ProblemDetails'
        - type: object
          properties:
            errors:
              type: object
              additionalProperties:
                type: array
                items: { type: string }
  responses:
    BadRequest:
      description: Validation failed
      content:
        application/problem+json:
          schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/ValidationProblem' }
paths:
  /users:
    post:
      responses:
        '400': { $ref: '#/components/responses/BadRequest' }

bytes.topic: Caching — HTTP cache headers, CDN, ETag and conditional requests
bytes.title: OpenAPI — Documenting HTTP Caching: ETags, Cache-Control, and 304 Responses
bytes.body: OpenAPI specs routinely omit caching semantics, leaving clients and CDNs to guess. Model them explicitly. For GET endpoints that support conditional requests, declare `If-None-Match` and `If-Modified-Since` as header parameters — this signals to generated clients and gateways that these inputs are intentional. Document `ETag` and `Cache-Control` in the `headers` object of your `200` response. The `ETag` value should reflect the weak/strong validator syntax (`"abc123"` or `W/"abc123"`). Always include a `304 Not Modified` response with no `content` key — OpenAPI 3.x treats its absence as an intentional empty body, which matches the HTTP spec exactly. For CDN-aware APIs, also document `Vary: Accept-Encoding, Accept` so shared caches know which dimensions produce distinct responses. Apply `Cache-Control: max-age=3600, must-revalidate` on mutable resources and `immutable` on versioned or content-addressed assets. Tooling like Redoc and SwaggerUI will surface these headers in generated docs, making caching contracts first-class API guarantees rather than implicit implementation details.
bytes.code_snippet:
paths:
  /articles/{id}:
    get:
      parameters:
        - name: If-None-Match
          in: header
          schema: { type: string }
        - name: If-Modified-Since
          in: header
          schema: { type: string, format: date-time }
      responses:
        '200':
          headers:
            ETag:
              schema: { type: string, example: '"d4f9a1"' }
            Cache-Control:
              schema: { type: string, example: 'max-age=3600, must-revalidate' }
            Vary:
              schema: { type: string, example: 'Accept-Encoding, Accept' }
          content:
            application/json:
              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/Article' }
        '304':
          description: Not Modified — client cache is still valid

bytes.topic: Testing — contract testing, mocking, integration tests per protocol
bytes.title: OpenAPI Contract Testing — Lock Your API Behavior with Schemathesis and Prism
bytes.body:
Contract testing in OpenAPI means treating your `openapi.yaml` as the source of truth and verifying that both your server and consumers honor it. Two tools own this space: **Schemathesis** for server-side property-based contract testing, and **Stoplight Prism** for mock-driven consumer testing.

Schemathesis reads your OpenAPI 3.x document and auto-generates hundreds of HTTP test cases using property-based fuzzing — it tests every path, validates response schemas against `components/schemas`, checks status codes, and flags undocumented responses. Run it in CI with `schemathesis run openapi.yaml --checks all --base-url http://localhost:5000`.

Prism spins up a mock server from your spec: `prism mock openapi.yaml`. Consumers write integration tests against this mock, guaranteeing they only call documented endpoints with valid request shapes. If the spec changes and breaks consumer tests, the contract is violated — caught before deployment.

The critical discipline: keep your spec as the *first* artifact, not a post-hoc annotation. Use `x-internal: true` to exclude private endpoints from contract surface. Pair Schemathesis with `--validate-schema=true` to catch spec authoring errors before they propagate.
bytes.code_snippet:
# CI contract test pipeline
# 1. Validate the spec itself
npx @redocly/cli lint openapi.yaml

# 2. Start your real server
dotnet run &

# 3. Run Schemathesis against live server
schemathesis run openapi.yaml \
  --base-url http://localhost:5000 \
  --checks all \
  --validate-schema=true \
  --hypothesis-max-examples=50

# 4. Spin up Prism mock for consumer tests
npx @stoplight/prism-cli mock openapi.yaml --port 4010 &
npm run test:integration  # tests hit :4010, not real server

bytes.topic: Security — input validation, CSRF, injection, GraphQL introspection risks
bytes.title: OpenAPI Security — Locking Down Input Validation, CSRF, and Injection at the Spec Layer
bytes.body: OpenAPI schemas are your first line of defense, but they only enforce what you wire up server-side. Use `pattern`, `minLength`, `maxLength`, and `enum` on every user-supplied string field — these constraints feed directly into validators like `express-openapi-validator` or `oapi-codegen` and block injection payloads before they hit business logic. For `format: uri` fields, add an explicit `pattern` to allowlist schemes (`^https://`) — otherwise you've opened an SSRF vector. Cookie-based `securitySchemes` (`in: cookie`) require `SameSite=Strict` and a CSRF double-submit token; OpenAPI lets you declare both a cookie scheme and a header scheme in tandem — enforce both in your middleware. Treat your `/openapi.json` endpoint like GraphQL introspection: gate it behind auth in production. Leaking full schema exposes every parameter name, format, and internal path to attackers. Use `x-internal: true` on sensitive operations and strip them at doc-generation time. Run `redocly lint` with `security-defined` rule enabled — it fails the build if any operation lacks a `security` declaration.
bytes.code_snippet:
# openapi.yaml — injection-hardened request body
requestBody:
  required: true
  content:
    application/json:
      schema:
        type: object
        required: [username, redirectUrl]
        properties:
          username:
            type: string
            minLength: 3
            maxLength: 32
            pattern: '^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$'   # blocks SQLi / XSS chars
          redirectUrl:
            type: string
            format: uri
            pattern: '^https://app\.example\.com/'  # prevents open redirect + SSRF

bytes.topic: Idempotency — safe vs idempotent methods, idempotency keys for mutations
bytes.title: OpenAPI Idempotency — Documenting Safe Methods and Idempotency Keys for POST Mutations
bytes.body:
OpenAPI's operation semantics map directly to RFC 9110 HTTP method properties. `GET`, `HEAD`, and `OPTIONS` are both safe and idempotent — document them with no requestBody and no side-effect responses. `PUT` and `DELETE` are idempotent but not safe; repeated calls produce the same server state, so your OpenAPI spec should reflect `200`/`204` on repeat without `201`. `POST` is neither — each call potentially creates a new resource.

For idempotent `POST` mutations (payments, order submissions), add an `Idempotency-Key` header parameter marked `required: true` with `schema.format: uuid`. Document two success responses: `201` for a new resource and `200` for a replayed response. This signals to SDK generators (openapi-generator, kiota) and API gateways that the operation supports client-controlled deduplication.

Server-side, store a hash of `(idempotency_key, request_body)` against the serialized response. Return the cached response if the key is seen again within the TTL. On key collision with a different body, return `422 Unprocessable Entity` — document this response in your spec so clients handle it explicitly.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Idempotent POST — same Idempotency-Key returns the cached response
KEY=$(uuidgen)

curl -X POST https://api.example.com/payments \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Idempotency-Key: $KEY" \
  -d '{"amount": 5000, "currency": "usd"}'
# → 201 Created

# Retry after network failure — no duplicate charge
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/payments \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Idempotency-Key: $KEY" \
  -d '{"amount": 5000, "currency": "usd"}'
# → 200 OK (replayed, not re-processed)

bytes.topic: Choosing a Protocol — REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC decision framework
bytes.title: OpenAPI Protocol Decision — REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC Decision Framework
bytes.body:
OpenAPI is REST-native: the moment you write an openapi.yaml, you've committed to HTTP semantics — paths, verbs, status codes, and schema-validated bodies. That commitment pays off when your API is resource-oriented, consumed by diverse clients, or needs a rich toolchain (Swagger UI, Prism mocking, OpenAPI Generator, Spectral linting).

Choose REST + OpenAPI when contracts are stable and discoverable. The components/schemas section gives you a single source of truth, and securitySchemes natively models OAuth2, OIDC, and API keys — no extra layer needed.

Choose GraphQL when consumers demand flexible querying across deeply nested resources, or when multiple front-ends need different projections of the same data. OpenAPI can't express a query language — its paths are fixed by definition.

Choose gRPC when you need bidirectional streaming, sub-millisecond latency, or tight internal service contracts via Protobuf. Notably, gRPC-gateway can emit an openapi.yaml from your .proto, bridging both worlds for HTTP/JSON consumers without rewriting the service.

The litmus test: if your API surface maps cleanly to CRUD over nouns with predictable schemas, REST + OpenAPI wins on tooling ROI alone.
bytes.code_snippet:
# REST + OpenAPI: generate type-safe client from contract
npx @openapitools/openapi-generator-cli generate \
  -i openapi.yaml \
  -g typescript-fetch \
  -o ./src/generated

# gRPC: generate from proto (no openapi.yaml)
protoc --go_out=. --go-grpc_out=. article.proto

# gRPC-gateway bridge: emit openapi.yaml FROM proto
# lets REST clients consume gRPC service via HTTP/JSON
protoc --openapiv2_out=./docs article.proto

bytes.topic: Performance — payload size, compression, connection reuse, multiplexing
bytes.title: OpenAPI Performance: Compression Headers, Content Negotiation, and $ref Bundle Strategy
bytes.body:
OpenAPI's `responses[code].headers` object is the authoritative place to declare compression behavior — not just for documentation, but because generators like `oapi-codegen` and `@hey-api/openapi-ts` wire declared headers into typed client interceptors. Declare `Content-Encoding` with an enum of `[gzip, br, identity]` and always co-document `Vary: Accept-Encoding` to prevent CDN cache poisoning on compressed responses.

For payload size, OpenAPI 3.1's `content` map supports multiple media types per operation. Registering both `application/json` and `application/msgpack` pointing to the same `$ref` schema lets negotiation-aware clients opt into binary encoding — typically 20–40% smaller than JSON — without duplicating your schema definition.

`$ref` reuse in `components/schemas` also directly reduces generated client bundle size: tools emit one TypeScript interface or Go struct per component, not one per inline occurrence. Inline schemas in 50-endpoint specs can balloon generated output by 3–5×.

HTTP/2 multiplexing isn't modeled in the OpenAPI spec itself, but document `Link` preload headers in responses — generators can surface these as typed accessors, enabling clients to pipeline dependent fetches without custom glue code.
bytes.code_snippet:
paths:
  /feed:
    get:
      responses:
        "200":
          headers:
            Content-Encoding:
              schema: { type: string, enum: [gzip, br, identity] }
            Vary:
              schema: { type: string, example: "Accept-Encoding, Accept" }
          content:
            application/json:
              schema: { $ref: "#/components/schemas/FeedResponse" }
            application/msgpack:
              schema: { $ref: "#/components/schemas/FeedResponse" }
